sales.title:
Ancients
PTOLEMAIC EGYPT. Ptolemy I Soter (305/4-282 BC). AR stater or tetradrachm (26mm, 14.28 gm, 1h). NGC Choice XF S 5/5 - 5/5, Fine Style. Alexandria, from 294 BC or shortly thereafter. Diademed head of Ptolemy I right, aegis tied around neck; Δ (engraver's signature) behind ear, dotted border / ΠTOΛEMAIOY-BAΣIΛEΩΣ, eagle standing left on thunderbolt, with closed wings; P / Φ in left field. CPE 151. Splendidly detailed example with saffron backlight. HID05401242017 © 2025 Heritage Auctions | All Rights Reserved www.HA.com/TexasAuctioneerLicenseNotice

PTOLEMAIC EGYPT. Ptolemy I Soter (305/4-282 BC). AR stater or tetradrachm (28mm, 15.35 gm, 4h). NGC XF 5/5 - 1/5, brushed, edge chips. Attic Standard, Alexandria, struck in the name of Alexander III the Great of Macedon, ca. 311-310 BC, obverse die signed by the artist Δ. Diademed head of Alexander III right, wearing elephant headdress, mitra, and scaly aegis tied before neck; small Δ (artist's signature) to the right of the elephant's ear in the scales, dotted border / AΛEΞΑΝΔPOY, Athena advancing right, brandishing spear in right hand, shield on raised left arm; EY and eagle standing right on thunderbolt to lower right, with closed wings. CPE 47. Svoronos 42. From The JGC Collection. HID05401242017 © 2025 Heritage Auctions | All Rights Reserved www.HA.com/TexasAuctioneerLicenseNotice
Ancients

PTOLEMAIC EGYPT. Ptolemy II Philadelphus (285/284-246 BC). AV trichryson or pentadrachm (24mm, 17.81 gm, 1h). NGC Choice AU 5/5 - 5/5, Fine Style. Alexandria, ca. 275/274-272 BC. Diademed head of Ptolemy I right, aegis tied around neck; dotted border / ΠTOΛEMAIOY-BAΣIΛEΩΣ, eagle standing left on thunderbolt, wings closed; ΣΩ monogram above Galatian shield in left field, AI monogram in right field, dotted border. CPE 304. Svoronos 551. Mirror-like surfaces with highlights of amber. Rare type.   From The Cambridge Collection. Ex The Lexington Collection of Jonathan K. Kern (Heritage Auctions, Auction 3035, 3 September 2014), lot 29123.   The trichryson, a gold piece of about 24mm and 17.8 grams (the approximate size and weight of an Attic silver tetradrachm), was first introduced by Ptolemy I shortly after he assumed the title of Basileus (king) in 305 BC. The weight was equivalent to five Ptolemaic silver drachms on the reduced Phoenician standard adopted by Ptolemy I (hence the modern term "pentadrachm" usually applied to this denomination) but under the Ptolemaic system the value would have been 12 times as great, or 60 silver drachms - a huge sum in the ancient world. It was the largest gold coin the world had yet seen, testament to the vast wealth of Egypt. The Alexandria mint employed the best engravers in the production of its gold coinage and the quality of portraiture is typically outstanding. The production of gold trichrysons continued under Ptolemy II Philadelphus until circa August 272 BC, when it was replaced by an even larger gold coin, the mnaieion, worth 100 silver drachms.   HID05401242017  © 2025 Heritage Auctions | All Rights Reserved www.HA.com/TexasAuctioneerLicenseNotice

sales.title:
PTOLEMAIC EGYPT. Ptolemy I Soter (305/4-282 BC). AR stater or tetradrachm (27mm, 15.69 gm, 11h). NGC AU 4/5 - 5/5, overstruck. Ptolemaic standard, Alexandria, struck in the name of Alexander III the Great, ca. 306-300 BC, obverse die signed by the artist Δ. Diademed head of Alexander III right, wearing elephant headdress, mitra, and scaly aegis tied before neck; small Δ (artist's signature) to the right of the elephant's ear in the scales, dotted border / AΛEΞΑΝΔPOY, Athena advancing right, brandishing spear in right hand, shield on raised left arm; ΔI monogram in inner right field, Corinthian helmet right above eagle right on thunderbolt to lower right. CPE 72. Svoronos 169. Ablaze with vibrant tones of amber and russet. Ex Münzen & Medaillen AG Basel, Auction 85 (11 April 1997), lot 147. HID05401242017 © 2025 Heritage Auctions | All Rights Reserved www.HA.com/TexasAuctioneerLicenseNotice
Ancients

SYRIA. Seleukid Kingdom. Demetrios I Soter, 162-150 B.C. AR Tetradrachm (16.67 gms), Antioch on the Orontes Mint, ca. 162-155/4 B.C. NGC Ch AU, Strike: 4/5 Surface: 4/5. Fine Style. Edge Chip, Die Shift, Brushed.
HGC-9, 795f; SC-1638.1g. Obverse: Diademed head right within laurel wreath border; Reverse: Tyche, holding scepter and cornucopia, seated left on backless throne with winged tritoness support; monogram to outer left. Struck on a very broad flan and in stunning high relief. Mottled charcoal tone with faint cleaning marks evident under magnification. An impressive example of the type.
From the Peter Jones M.D. Collection (Greek Coin Art pg. 227; #2516).


To view all items from the Peter Jones M.D. Collection, click here.
